posts.text: We lost 50% of our frontline staff every year. Think about that number. That means for every two people we employed, one of them left every year on average. In just two years, we’d turn over enough people to have an entirely new workforce, mathematically. And this nightmare turnover didn’t happen in a warehouse, call center, or retail operation. These were direct care workers. People showing up at 6am to help adults with developmental disabilities get dressed, eat breakfast, and start their day. They did it for minimal pay in a 24/7 operation all year long. And we couldn't keep them. I worked in HR for that company. The hardest part wasn't the turnover number. It was watching the cycle repeat itself over and over with no way to get ahead of it. Someone would start on a Monday. No structured onboarding. No real introduction to the team or the mission. By Friday, they were gone. The managers had nearly stopped caring. You can only watch so many people walk out the door before you start assuming the next one will, too. We were always reacting. Always backfilling. Always putting out fires that better processes might have prevented. I remembered that time  when I looked at Cue, the frontline intelligence Fountain just launched. It's not another dashboard that tells you how bad your retention numbers are. Cue actually runs and supports the operations: hiring, onboarding, scheduling, workforce management. You describe what you need. It proposes a plan. You approve. It executes. And HR teams save their time for designing higher-touch employee experiences, career growth plans, and retention strategies. For the teams I used to work with, the ones managing 20 locations, constant no-shows, and workers who never got a real shot at staying, that kind of proactive infrastructure would have changed everything. The people doing the hardest work deserve better than reactive HR. Check out Cue: fountain.com/cue #FrontlineSuperintelligence #FountainCue #HRtech #FutureOfWork #DirectCare #Ad | Paid partnership with @Fountain
posts.hook: We lost 50% of our frontline staff every year.
posts.name: Ben Eubanks

posts.text: On Thursday, June 25th at 11AM EST join us for, The Customer Education Gap: What Separates High-Performing Programs from the Rest, a webinar that includes benchmark data from over 250+ programs: https://absorbl.ms/uqDSw Debbie Smith from CEdMA and Ben Eubanks from Lighthouse Research & Advisory host this session that will include takeaways such as: 💡 Why Customer Success and Support teams outperform marketing as participation drivers 💡 Where AI adoption is happening in customer education and where the biggest untapped opportunities remain 💡 The operational patterns shared by programs with the highest learner return rates and strongest leadership support ..and more! This is a must-see for your customer education teams. We hope to see you there. 😃

posts.text: My SHRM Conference 2026 Experience: Earlier this year, I signed up to go to the SHRM26 Conference in Orlando. This is something I have wanted to attend for over a decade. I was so excited to network and learn. Unfortunately, tragedy struck my family, and my husband was on life support for about ten days in late April. He miraculously survived, but not without many health complications we are still sorting out today. I am his caretaker, and with this unfortunate event, it became evident at his last checkup that he would not be well enough to care for himself, let alone our children, for a week… and I was no longer in a position to attend SHRM26. After this checkup, I emailed SHRM’s customer experience and let them know, in painful detail, of my situation and asked for a way I can either cancel with a refund, or transfer my credits to next year’s conference. The answer? No. No BUT you can attend a two day conference in Nashville that is not nearly the value. No BUT you can attend virtually. No BUT you can send a substitute. An organization that preaches valuing people… choosing not to have any empathy for humans with real tragic situations. With everything going on in my life (that doesn’t need aired in detail on LinkedIn), I could have used one humane interaction – “Sure, we can transfer the credits to next year’s event.” I believe, as individuals who work within the HR field, we need to have a conscious awareness of which organizations we support, and how they represent the values that we should champion when it comes to being fair and humane to those we work with. With the recent allegations and legal trouble SHRM has been in, I wanted to add a bit of personal experience to help paint a bigger picture of how they treat individuals. I don’t believe SHRM is the organization it used to be. And I don’t think HR professionals should be supporting it. Not just because of my situation, but because of the organization they have proven themselves to be, over and over again. SHRM Johnny C. Taylor, Jr., SHRM-SCP
posts.hook: My SHRM Conference 2026 Experience: Earlier this year, I signed up to go to the SHRM26 Conference in Orlando.
posts.name: Ben Eubanks

posts.text: What advice do you give your children that play sports? At isolved Connect today in Vegas. Loving watching Pragya Gupta interview Julie Foudy, US Women's Soccer legend. Julie talked about how in the world of AI the people have to set the tone and the culture. Plus some wonderful stories about motherhood, soccer, and more. Her advice fits so many things in life. Do what you enjoy. Do what you love. Life is too short to do otherwise. ♥️ My thing I always say to my kids before any competition? Have FUN. When it stops being fun, we'll find something else to do. Until then, enjoy! Good reminder for all of us adults today.
posts.hook: What advice do you give your children that play sports?
posts.name: Ben Eubanks

posts.text: Breaking up your training into short, social media-like videos? New research says that might not be an effective strategy. A big trend in learning content over the last decade has been "chunkable" or "bite sized" videos. But there's a limit to that, as new evidence shows. "Researchers at Yunnan Normal University and Central China Normal University recently set out to assess the potential of short videos and longer videos as learning resources. Their findings, published in Communications Psychology, suggest that short videos are significantly less effective learning tools than longer videos, as people tend to rapidly forget the information presented in them." "Across three experiments, memory performance and forgetting rate were assessed under both incidental and intentional encoding conditions, and inter-subject correlation (ISC) analysis was employed to investigate neural response patterns during short video viewing," wrote the authors. "Behaviorally, participants learning with short videos showed significantly lower immediate memory accuracy across encoding conditions and exhibited a higher rate of forgetting when explicitly instructed to remember." This type of short form video is hard for the brain to process because it breaks up attention and memory storage. Takeaway: the shorter the content gets (experiment tested content as short as 90 seconds) the less likely viewers (learners!) will be able to learn, process, and remember the content. Source: phys .org
posts.hook: Breaking up your training into short, social media-like videos?
posts.name: Ben Eubanks

posts.text: Two years ago I stood on the stage at Adobe Learning Summit and told a group of learning leaders exactly what their learners were thinking. We explored topics like: ---> Their preferred sources of learning content (hint: not the LMS!) ---> The two crucial elements for a strong learning culture (opportunity + autonomy is the secret sauce) ---> How someone's generation/life stage affects their preferred source of learning content This June I'll be back again to share updated research on these topics! We're analyzing the data now and the findings are fascinating. AI is changing WHAT people learn, HOW people learn, and WHY people learn. But there are other important changes that we can't miss, too. If you're trying to create a learning environment and culture where your people can win, this is a session you're going to want to see. Come join me on June 24th and 25th in Las Vegas and let's spend time with leaders who are building, testing, and rethinking what learning looks like inside their organizations today. Sign up for Adobe Learning Summit here: https://lnkd.in/diKS5svz #AdobeLearningSummit #LearningStrategy Adobe Learning Manager
posts.hook: Two years ago I stood on the stage at Adobe Learning Summit and told a group of learning leaders exactly what their learners were thinking.

posts.text: Today, our research shows that 40% of workers are either planning to quit or uncertain about their future with their employer. And the research tells us the danger isn't just that people might leave. It's that they check out mentally long before they ever hand in a resignation. Next week I'll be joining the team at Insperity for the second half of our webinar series on #employeeexperience. Specifically we're looking at #employeeretention and how managers play a role in winning (or losing) that battle. I'm excited to learn from our all-star panel! Ashley Bonilla with insights on how we hire, screen, and onboard great people Dana St. Pierre-Cabrera, a people leader who intentionally creates environments to listen, recognize, develop, and support the workforce Michael Zeglinski who will share from the CEO perspective about making culture and values REAL and tangible, not just a poster on the wall This has been such a fun set of events with the Insperity team and the audience loved the last one. Want to join? Here's the link to sign up: https://lnkd.in/gQDZTwei Dianne Chatterton, MS
posts.hook: Today, our research shows that 40% of workers are either planning to quit or uncertain about their future with their employer.

posts.text: "We're in a time where the value of humanity has never been higher." Jim Morgan Today we wrapped up our last recordings for HR Summer School. Next week we go live Tuesday through Thursday. So many amazing conversations coming. Allyson Smart, SPHR Kamaria Scott Angela N. Briggs-Paige, CMBA, SPHR, CDR Lance Sapera Zech Dahms, MBA Shane Noe Mike Bollinger Tyrese Manigault Christine Beldner, MSHRM, SPHR, SHRM-SCP Kenneth Matos Emma Belconis Jack Hooper Amy Mosher Abhijit Bhaduri Each of these people is hand picked for what they bring to the conversation, and there's so much good stuff ahead! George Rogers and I spend months planning this and we're excited to see everyone in the community come together and celebrate the humans that make work happen Want to join us? HRSummerSchool is easy to find. 😉 It's free. 7.5 HRCI/SHRM credits. A prize closet chock full of goodies to give away! Most importantly, a community that celebrates the successes of everyone, because we're in this together. 🫂 Plus we will give away this year's Unity award to someone who is going above and beyond to support, lift others up, and contribute to the community in a meaningful way. Grateful to Employ Cornerstone OnDemand isolved Take Command HiBob Verified First for supporting this event and making a positive impact on the #hrsummerschool family. ♥️
